title: Azure Logic Apps - Retries, Run-After, And Error-Handling Patterns
applies_to: Reliable Logic Apps workflows that call APIs and persist data
Logic Apps error handling, retries, and control-flow design

confirmed_facts.fact: Logic Apps supports configuring an action's run-after behavior so downstream actions can run after success, failure, skipped, or timed-out states from previous actions.
Scopes can group actions and are commonly used with run-after behavior to implement try/catch/finally-style workflow patterns.
Logic Apps supports retry policies for actions such as HTTP calls, including default exponential retry behavior and configurable retry settings.
Trigger and action settings can be changed in the workflow definition language, including runtime configuration that affects execution behavior.

implementation_notes: For API ingestion workflows, place the source API call and parsing steps inside a scope, then use separate scopes for success handling, failure capture, and cleanup or notification paths.
Use explicit retry policies for external API calls instead of relying blindly on defaults, especially when the source API documents its own rate limits or retry-after behavior.
When writing to SharePoint, separate retrieval and persistence scopes so failed writes can be handled independently of retrieval failures.
Use run-after conditions to prevent one bad row or one downstream write failure from obscuring where the real fault occurred.
caveats: Retries can create duplicates if the target write operation is not idempotent.
A workflow that retries source reads and target writes without a source key or deduplication strategy can corrupt SharePoint data over time.
Error handling in the designer is easy to misread unless the intended run-after graph is documented clearly.
